text: On the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram, where do main-sequence stars spend most of their lives?
choices: A) Along the main sequence band
B) In the red giant branch
C) In the white dwarf region
explanation: Main-sequence stars fuse hydrogen in their cores; this phase is the longest and most stable stage of stellar life.

text: What is the final remnant left by a low-mass star (like the Sun) after it exhausts its nuclear fuel?
explanation: Low-mass stars shed their outer layers as a planetary nebula and leave behind an Earth-sized white dwarf composed mostly of carbon and oxygen.

text: Red giants are cooler and more luminous than main-sequence stars of the same mass.
explanation: As a star evolves off the main sequence its outer layers expand and cool, drastically increasing luminosity despite lower surface temperature.

text: What is the approximate age of the Universe according to current cosmological measurements?
explanation: Measurements of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) by the Planck satellite place the age of the Universe at approximately 13.8 billion years.

text: Dark energy makes up approximately 68% of the total energy content of the Universe.
explanation: According to the standard ΛCDM model, dark energy (~68%), dark matter (~27%), and ordinary matter (~5%) compose the total energy-matter budget.

text: Which observation first provided direct evidence that the expansion of the Universe is accelerating?
choices: B) Type Ia supernova distances in 1998
explanation: In 1998 two independent teams (Perlmutter et al.; Riess et al.) found Type Ia supernovae were dimmer than expected, proving the expansion is speeding up.

text: Which planet in the Solar System has the largest number of confirmed moons?
explanation: Saturn currently holds the record with over 140 confirmed moons, surpassing Jupiter following recent discoveries announced in 2023.

text: The Oort Cloud is thought to be the source of which type of Solar System objects?
explanation: The spherical Oort Cloud extends from ~2,000 to 100,000 AU and is the reservoir for long-period comets (orbital periods > 200 years).

text: Which type of telescope uses a primary mirror to collect and focus light?
explanation: Reflector (reflecting) telescopes use a curved primary mirror. Refractors use lenses; both collect visible light, but large mirrors are cheaper and avoid chromatic aberration.

text: The James Webb Space Telescope primarily observes in infrared wavelengths.
explanation: JWST is optimised for near- and mid-infrared (0.6–28 μm), allowing it to observe high-redshift galaxies and peer through dust clouds.

text: Which of the following best describes an elliptical galaxy?
choices: A) Flat disk with spiral arms and active star formation
B) Smooth, featureless ellipsoidal shape with little gas or dust
C) Irregular shape with prominent HII regions
D) Barred disk with a central ring
explanation: Elliptical galaxies (Hubble type E) are pressure-supported spheroids containing old stellar populations and very little interstellar gas.

text: What powers the extreme luminosity of a quasar?
choices: B) Accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole
explanation: Quasars are active galactic nuclei where infalling gas heats to millions of degrees in the accretion disk around a supermassive black hole (10⁶–10¹⁰ M☉).
